coredata.dc:title: Surface modification and ratiometric fluorescence dual function enhancement for visual and fluorescent detection of glucose based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Tongfan
Wei, Xiao
Nie, Yijing
Xu, Yeqing
Lu, Kai
Yan, Yongsheng
Zhou, Zhiping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) functionalized dual-emission quantum dots (QDs) were prepared and utilized as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the onsite naked eye determination of glucose. The unique ratiometric fluorescence probe, which combined ratiometric fluorescence technique with boric acid functional materials into one system, has been prepared by hybridizing red-emitting CdTe QDs (r-QDs) and green-emitting CdTe QDs (g-QDs). The fluorescence of the embedded r-QDs stays constant, whereas the g-QDs functionalized with APBA can selectively bind glucose by the chemistry of boronic acid and cis diol compounds, leading to the fluorescence of g-QDs quenched due to the surface quenching states induced mechanism and resulting in continuous fluorescence color changes (from green to red) of the ratiometric fluorescence probe system. Under optimum conditions, the proposed ratiometric probe was used for selective detection of glucose in a concentration range of 0.1–2.0mmol/L with a detection limit of 4.5μmol/L. Moreover, the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in human serum samples. The present study provides an efficient and facile ratiometric fluorescence probe for rapid, convenient, sensitive and selective visual identification of glucose in blood glucose sensing without the need of complex equipment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
3-Aminophenylboronic acid
Selective Recognition
Visual detection
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CdTe QDs and r-QDs@SiO2 nanospheres
Preparation of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
Fluorescent and visual detection of glucose
Synthesis of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
Analytical performance of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
Selectivity of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
Detection of glucose in human serum samples

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe and the sensing mechanism of glucose.
Fluorescence emission spectra of g-QDs (1), r-QDs@SiO2 (2), and r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA (3). Inset: the photos of the corresponding fluorescence colors under UV lamp (λ ex =365nm).
TEM images of r-QDs@SiO2 (a) and r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA (b).

Fluorescence spectra, linear relationships and the corresponding photographs of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA system (a), r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs system (b) and g-QDs@APBA system (c) upon the exposure to different concentrations of glucose. And the selectivity of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe to other saccharides (d).
Results of glucose determination in human serum samples.
Comparison between present method and other boronic acid based fluorescent nano-sensing method.

row.entry: Boronic acid function and dual-emission QDs

head.ce:title: Surface modification and ratiometric fluorescence dual function enhancement for visual and fluorescent detection of glucose based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) functionalized dual-emission quantum dots (QDs) were prepared and utilized as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the onsite naked eye determination of glucose. The unique ratiometric fluorescence probe, which combined ratiometric fluorescence technique with boric acid functional materials into one system, has been prepared by hybridizing red-emitting CdTe QDs (r-QDs) and green-emitting CdTe QDs (g-QDs). The fluorescence of the embedded r-QDs stays constant, whereas the g-QDs functionalized with APBA can selectively bind glucose by the chemistry of boronic acid and cis diol compounds, leading to the fluorescence of g-QDs quenched due to the surface quenching states induced mechanism and resulting in continuous fluorescence color changes (from green to red) of the ratiometric fluorescence probe system. Under optimum conditions, the proposed ratiometric probe was used for selective detection of glucose in a concentration range of 0.1–2.0mmol/L with a detection limit of 4.5μmol/L. Moreover, the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in human serum samples. The present study provides an efficient and facile ratiometric fluorescence probe for rapid, convenient, sensitive and selective visual identification of glucose in blood glucose sensing without the need of complex equipment.

ce:section.ce:para: Blood glucose sensing has attracted much attention in recent years due to the increasing severity of the diabetes mellitus. According to the World Health Organization statistics, there are about 300 million people with diabetes in 2010, and this number will be increased to almost double in the year of 2030 [1,2]. Glucose has become a commonly tested analyte because millions of diabetics need to measure the blood glucose levels everyday [3]. To date, various methods such as electrochemical approach and fluorescence analysis have been developed for glucose sensing [4,5]. Compared with well-developed electrochemical approach, fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention due to its simplicity, good stability, low cost, high sensitivity and test rapidity [6]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop rapid, facile, sensitive and selective fluorescence analytical methods to monitor the level of glucose in blood.

Quantum dots (QDs), also called semiconductor nanocrystals, have attracted continuous concern in recent years due to their excellent properties, such as good photostability, narrow and symmetric emission, broad absorption spectrum, and high luminescence efficiency [7–9]. Fluorescence methods based QDs have appeared as satisfactory techniques due to their low cost, rapid response, high sensitivity, and low detection limit, meanwhile, QDs are widely used as fluorescence sensors for sensing and detecting various analytes, such as ions, small molecules, and biological macromolecule [10–18]. For detecting glucose, there are two sensing systems based QDs-fluorescence method: one is the enzymatic system by using the enzyme glucose oxidases, the other is the nonenzymatic system based on the surface chemical properties of the QDs for glucose [19]. Compared with the former, the latter is even simpler, cheaper, faster and more stable. To our knowledge, the detection system based on the chemistry of boronic acid and cis diol compounds, a kind of nonenzymatic system, has been reported and successfully used as a special recognition way in sensor applications [20–24]. However, the boronic acid modified QDs-fluorescent sensors for blood glucose sensing are very few.

Recently, the ratiometric fluorescent technique has attracted continuous concern due to its advantages including improved sensitivity and accuracy at low concentration levels of analyte, and built-in corrections for the environmental effects [25–27]. The ratiometric fluorescent methods are mainly based on the measurement of changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two different wavelengths before and following optical detection [28]. In comparison with the single fluorescence probe, the color changes of the ratiometric fluorescent probe can be clearly and easily distinguished by the naked eyes [7]. Among the various ratiometric sensors, QDs-ratiometric fluorescent sensors possess many advantages as we mentioned already in the previous section. And there have been some significant reports about the QDs-ratiometric fluorescent sensors and their applications [29–36]. For example, Zhang et al. have reported a dual-emission QDs probe for visual detection of TNT particulates [29]. Yao et al. have reported a ratiometric fluorescence probe by dual-emission QDs for on-site visual determination of copper ions [30]. Wang et al. have prepared a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe with desired intensity ratio for onsite naked eye determination of cysteine and homocysteine [31]. It could be found from the literatures that the ratiometric fluorescence QDs probes could achieve high sensitivity with a lower detection limit and visual signal output observed by the naked eye. More importantly, dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probes are obtained by bonding two differently sized QDs in one nanoparticle, one of the QDs as a signal response unit and another as the reference. Therefore, dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe could be used as a powerful tool in biological and chemical sensing application.

In this work, we demonstrate a new concept to use the 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA) functionalized dual-emission QDs as the ratiometric fluorescence probe for the onsite naked eye determination of glucose. Firstly, thioglycollic acid (TGA)-capped CdTe QDs were directly synthesized with desired size via a conventional reflux method. Then the red-emitting CdTe QDs (r-QDs) were embedded in the silica nanoparticle by the reverse microemulsion method while the green-emitting CdTe QDs (g-QDs) were attached to the surface of the silica nanoparticle. Finally, APBA was covalently linked to the g-QDs and the APBA functionalized dual-emission QDs were obtained. The morphology, characteristic, optical stability, fluorescence property and selective recognition of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe was investigated. Further, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probe was demonstrated as an efficient and simple detecting system for selective detection of glucose in real blood samples. Notably, the APBA functionalized dual-emission QDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probe constructed in this work has several features for the determination of glucose: (1) the r-QDs were entrapped in the SiO2, which could provide a stable luminescent core and a reliable reference signal; (2) the g-QDs were modified by the APBA which could reacted with the glucose molecule by the covalent interaction, hence making the highly selective detection of glucose possible; (3) due to the variations of the intensity ratios of the dual-emission QDs, continuous color changes from green to red could be clearly and easily observed by naked eye. To the best of our knowledge, the boric acid functional QDs-ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing glucose has not been reported to date.

NaBH4 (99%), tellurium powder (∼100mesh, 99.99%), thioglycolic acid (TGA) (98%), CdCl2·2.5H2O (99.99%), Triton X-100, hexyl alcohol, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ammonia solution (25–28%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), poly (diallyldi-methyl-ammonium chloride) (PDDA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), APBA, glucose, fructose, galactose, and mannose were all purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cyclohexane, acetone, ethanol, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Double distilled water was used throughout.

The morphological evaluation was carried out by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (USA) equipped with a plotter unit and a quartz cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdTe QDs and r-QDs@SiO2 nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTe QDs in aqueous phase were synthesized according to previous reports [37]. Intially, 0.06g of NaBH4 and 0.051g of tellurium powder were added to 3.0mL of ultrapure water in a centrifuge tube under the ultrasonic condition to produce a clear NaHTe solution. Then, the fresh NaHTe solution was transferred into a mixed solution of CdCl2 and TGA at pH 11.2 under N2 atmosphere. The Cd2+/TGA/HTe− solution, which mole ratio was 1:2.4:0.5, was refluxed at 100°C under open-air conditions. The two differently colored CdTe QDs were synthesized at different refluxing times, and the sizes of them were about 2.3nm and 4.8nm, respectively. All the QDs used in this work were synthesized using this method and capped by TGA. In addition, the concentration of the CdTe QDs solution was 8.0mmolL−1 (according to Cd2+).

The r-QDs were entrapped in the silica nanospheres by a modified reverse microemulsion method [38]. Briefly, 800μL r-QDs were added to a mixed solution including 15mL of cyclohexane, 3.6mL of n-hexanol, and 3.6mL of triton X–100. After stirring for 30min, 200μL of ammonia and 120μL of PDDA solution (0.075% v/v) were added and stirred for another 30min. Then, 200μL of TEOS was introduced to the microemulsion system, and the mixture was stirred for 24h. Finally, the microemulsion system was broken by adding a certain volume of acetone. After removal of the supernatant, the r-QDs@SiO2 was obtained following washing, centrifugation, and drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, a certain amount of r-QDs@SiO2, 10mL of water and 20mL of PDDA solution (1%, v/v) were added to a 100mL flask. After stirring for 1h at room temperature, the r-QDs@SiO2-PDDA nanoparticles were obtained. After centrifugation and washing, the r-QDs@SiO2-PDDA nanoparticles were redispersed in 20mL of water, and then 2.0mL of the g-QDs were added to the solution. After stirring for 2h and being purified, the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs was obtained and redispersed in 10mL of Tris-HCl buffer. Subsequently, 10mL of EDC/NHS (4mg/L for each) was added to the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs solution and stirred for 15min, and then 20mg of APBA was added to the mixture solution. After vigorously stirring for 6h at room temperature in the dark and being purified, the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and visual detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength was set at 330nm with photomultiplier tube voltage at 800V. The slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a recording emission range of 450–750nm. The r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probes were dispersed in water to form the probe stock solution (2.0mg/mL). Glucose, fructose, galactose, and mannose were dissolved in water to obtain the analyte stock solutions (20mmol/L), respectively. A certain volume of the as-prepared r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe, different amounts of known concentration glucose standard solution, 1.0mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1mol/L) were added into a 5.0mL calibrated test tube, and then, the mixture was diluted to the mark with water and mixed thoroughly. After a reasonable time for incubation, the solution was transferred into the quartz cell for the fluorescence detection of glucose. To carry out the visual detection of glucose, part of the detection solutions (1.5mL) were transferred to a series of centrifuge tubes and the images of the color changes were obtained using a camera under UV illumination (excitation wavelength at 365nm) in a dark environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure and visual principle of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe are shown in Fig. 1 . First, the r-QDs were entrapped in the silica nanospheres by a modified reverse microemulsion method to get the r-QDs@SiO2 nanoparticles. In this method, cyclohexane, tritonX-100 and n-hexanol were used as the continuous phase, surfactant and co-surfactant, respectively. As shown in our previous work, only one QD can be encapsulated in the silica nanosphere by using reverse microemulsion method, which may obtain QD@SiO2 with weak fluorescence intensity and limited applications [38]. This may be attributed to the electrostatic repulsive force between the negatively charged CdTe QDs and the negatively charged silica oligomers during the hydrolysis process of TEOS. In order to solve this problem and get superior r-QDs@SiO2 with high fluorescence intensity, PDDA, a kind of polycation, was introduced to the reverse microemulsion system. The electrostatic interactions in the reaction environment could be balanced by using PDDA due to the existence of quaternary ammonium cations. Subsequently, the as-prepared r-QDs@SiO2 was modified by PDDA, making the surface of the nanoparticles filled with positively charges. After that, the negatively charged g-QDs were then bonded electrostatically on the surface of the r-QDs@SiO2 to get the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs. Finally, APBA molecules were covalently linked to the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs in the presence of EDC and NHS via the reaction between amino group and carboxyl group to form the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe. It has been reported that the boronic acid groups anchored on the surface of QDs could bond with glucose to form stable boronate complexes [20]. Inspired by this special interaction, we have posed the concept of visual detection of glucose based on the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe for the first time. The working principle for the detection of glucose can be described as follow: when detection of glucose, the fluorescence of the APBA modified g-QDs on the surface of the r-QDs@SiO2 can be selectively quenched, whereas the fluorescence of the r-QDs embedded in SiO2 remains stable.

The appropriate ratio of the two differently colored QDs played an important role in designing the unique ratiometric probe with high color difference acuity for the visual detection of glucose, so a series of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probes with different ratios were synthesized. As shown in Fig. S1, the ratio of the dual-emission peaks was quite different from each other due to the different additions of the two differently colored QDs. Moreover, when the volume ratio of g-QDs and r-QDs was 6:1, the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe would display obvious color changes with a small increase of glucose concentration. Therefore, the ratiometric probe with the desired fluorescence intensity ratio was chosen for further experiment.

The fluorescence spectra of g-QDs, r-QDs@SiO2, and r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe are shown in Fig. 2 . The g-QDs show a strong green fluorescence (under the UV lamp, λ =365nm) with the maximal emission wavelength at 530nm, and the r-QDs@SiO2 exhibits a strong red fluorescence with the maximal emission wavelength at 660nm. It could be found that the obtained r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA possessed well-resolved dual emission peaks at 660nm and 530nm, which were attributed to the constituent parts (g-QDs and r-QDs). Moreover, the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe shows a strong green fluorescence, indicating that the unique probe can be applied in the following visual detection of glucose.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, CdTe QDs were embedded in the silica nanoparticles successfully by reverse microemulsion method. It could be found that there were several QDs in each SiO2 nanoparticle via the use of PDDA, and the size of the r-QDs@SiO2 was about 60nm. After the two processes (electrostatics binding process and covalent linking process), a crude surface with many dark spots (g-QDs) was observed for the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA (as shown in Fig. 3b), indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence probes were prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The dose response of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe to glucose was examined. First of all, some important factors such as pH, optical stability and response time, which may affect the detection effect, were confirmed and optimized for the next fluorescence and visual detection. As we know, the optimum pH is about 7.4 for the detection of glucose by using boric acid groups. [20] So a pH of 7.4 was chosen in this work. To our knowledge, the optical stability of the ratiometric probe plays an important role in the fluorescence and visual detection, and it is necessary to study the optical stability before the analytical method has been established. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the dual emission QDs (Fg/Fr) was considerably stable for 13 times tests within 120min. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. S3, it increased a little in the first three days, after that, it began to decrease slowly in the next seven days. Based on the above researches, it could be found that the ratiometric probe had good stability and could be used for the detection of target. Although the reaction between QDs and target molecule is very fast and usually accomplished in a few seconds, the optimal response time of the glucose detection system was still need to be determined which could make the results more accurate and scientific. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the ratiometric probe with glucose was calculated at different times. For the first two minutes, the fluorescence intensity ratio decreased rapidly, and after that, it remained stable. Therefore, 2min was chosen as the optimal detection time for the glucose detection.

In the absence of glucose, the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe emits two obvious emission peaks at 530 and 660nm, which can be attributed to the fluorescence of g-QDs and r-QDs, respectively. Upon the addition of glucose, the intensity at 530nm from the g-QDs was gradually decreased while the fluorescence intensity at 660nm from the r-QDs remained constant, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence intensity ratio was closely related to the concentration of glucose, ranging from 0.1mmol/L to 2.0mmol/L, which could be used for the determination of glucose. The linear curve fitted an equation of log(Fg/Fr)0/(Fg/Fr)=0.44429 (Cglucose/mM)+0.01624 (R 2 =0.9941) with a detection limit of 4.5μM (S/N=3) (Fig. S5). Due to the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two different emission peaks, continuous color variations of the ratiometric probe solution with different concentrations of glucose could be observed, as demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 4a. Clearly, even a slight decrease of the intensity of the g-QDs could lead to obviously distinguishable color variations from the original background. Hence, the visual detection of glucose by the naked eye is feasible.

In order to verify the advantages of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe, r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs and g-QDs@APBA were also prepared as contrasts. For the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe, the surface of the ratiometric probe was carboxyl group which was quite different from boronic acid group. The response of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs ratiometric probe to glucose was also studied. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs could be quenched by glucose, but the quenching efficiency was much lower at the same detectable level, meanwhile, the color changes were not obviously. This result can be explained as no specific recognition chemical groups existed in the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs, so glucose could not bond on the surface of QDs and only part of the QDs were quenched. For the g-QDs@APBA fluorescence probe, only the single green-emitting APBA@CdTe QDs were used for the fluorescence detection of glucose, as shown in Fig. 4c. It could be found that, unlike the ratiometric fluorescence probe, the color changes of the green-emitting APBA@CdTe QDs probe solutions with different concentrations of glucose were hard to observe by the naked eye. The comparison between the single fluorescence probe and ratiometric fluorescence probe clearly indicated that the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe was more reliable and sensitive for the visual detection of glucose than the single fluorescence probe, although the fluorescence intensity of the green-emitting APBA@CdTe QDs decreased at the same level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: Several kinds of structurally related to glucose (namely, fructose, galactose, and mannose) were used to evaluate the selectivity of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe. The fluorescence intensity ratios of the ratiometric probe were recorded (Fg/Fr) in the presence of glucose and other saccharides. As shown in Fig. 4d, the [(Fg/Fr)0/(Fg/Fr)] of the ratiometric fluorescence probe solution was about 4.03 by glucose (c =1.3mM), which was accompanied by obvious color changes of the probe solution under the UV lamp. In contrast, only little changes happened in the intensity ratio and fluorescence color after adding other saccharides in the ratiometric probe solution. The results implied the high selectivity of the r-QDs@SiO2@g-QDs@APBA ratiometric fluorescence probe for recognition of glucose over other saccharides. The phenomenon can be reasonably explained as follows: The APBA modified g-QDs on the surface of the r-QDs@SiO2 were quenched by glucose due to the specific covalent-binding reaction of boronic acid groups with the two pairs of cis diols of glucose. Clearly, the existence of two pairs of cis diols in glucose was the key structural feature that conferred a glucose-specific response on APBA modified g-QDs. Compared with glucose, other saccharides (fructose, galactose and mannose) had no such special structure to further bind with APBA modified g-QDs to form aggregates. This is why only little changes in fluorescence intensity ratio and color were observed at high concentration of other saccharides. This unique glucose-pecific response on APBA modified g-QDs essentially leads to the high selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence probe for glucose over other saccharides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the practical application of the proposed method, the ratiometric fluorescence probe was applied to the determination of glucose in human serum samples. Before the determination, the serum samples were diluted (10 fold) by the Tris-HCl (50mM, pH 7.4). Then the samples were spiked with standard glucose, and the results were shown in Table 1 . It could be found that the recoveries of glucose and the RSD were satisfactory, indicating that the proposed sensor could be used as an effective tool for direct and rapid fluorescence detection of glucose in human serum samples.

Much excellent works about the boronic acid functionalized fluorescence nanoprobes for detection of glucose have been reported in recent years. Some of them were summarized and compared with the present work in Table 2 . It could be found that both of the linear range and the detection limit are lower than other detection methods. Compared with other boronic acid based fluorescent nano-sensing methods, the present detection method not only possessed a higher sensitivity for glucose, but also provided a new concept for selective visual detection of glucose. In addition, the color change of the ratiometric fluorescent probe could be became a new standard for the glucose monitoring.

In summary, a novel ABPA functionalized dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe was fabricated by haybridization of two differently colored CdTe QDs for the onsite naked eye determination of glucose. By combining the superior sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence technique and high selectivity of boric acid groups, the ABPA functionalized dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe demonstrated specific recognition and sensitive visual detection ability for glucose. The presence of glucose in solutions could be observed by the fluorescence color changes of the probe solutions. The ratiometric probe was also used to quantify glucose by the measurement of ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the probe solutions. Compared with the single APBA functionalized QD-based probe and common dual-emission QDs ratiometric fluorescence probe, this probe exhibited obviously increased visual detection sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in human serum samples, which verify the feasibility and reliability of visual detection of glucose in complex environments. The simple, rapid, convenient, and reliable ABPA functionalized dual-emission QDs ratiometric probe sensing approach provided a new concept and an attractive idea for glucose monitoring, and it could be extended to other substance detection via properly functionalizing the response QDs. In addition, the proposed probes assembled on various substrates for the practical applications are in progress in our laboratory.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21277063, No. 21407057, No. 21407064 and No. 21446015), National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, 2012CB821500), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20140535), Ph.D. Innovation Programs Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. KYLX_1032), National Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2014M561595), Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded Project of Jiangsu Province (No. 1401108C).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.02.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: TongfanHao was born in 1988. She has been a doctoral candidate since 2014, School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on the field of optical sensors.

XiaoWei was born in 1988. He has been a doctoral candidate since 2013, School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interests focus mainly on the field of optical sensors.

YijingNie was born in 1985. He obtained his Ph.D. in Polymer Chemistry and Physics (2013) from Nanjing University. Now he is a lecturer at School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His current research interests focus on computer simulations and the preparation of functional polymers.

YeqingXu was born in 1992. She has been a master candidate since 2014, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. Her research interests focus mainly on the field of optical sensors.

KaiLu was born in 1991. He has been a master candidate since 2014, School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. His research interests focus mainly on the field of optical sensors.

YongshengYan, professor of Chemistry at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. He received his master’s degree from Hebei University (China) and Ph.D. from Huazhong University (China), in 1994 and 2003 respectively. His research interests concentrate in designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry, energy, and environment.

ZhipingZhou, professor of Material at School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, China. He received his master’s degree from Hangzhou University (China) and Ph.D. from Shanghai Jiaotong University (China), in 1989 and 1998 respectively. His research interests concentrate in designing and preparation of functional nanomaterials and their applications in chemistry, energy, and environment.
